sentences.text: OK, my name is Hilde Scheraga-- my married name.
I was born in Frankfurt, Germany-- Frankfurt am Main-- on January the 29th, 1930.
I, I came from a fairly traditional family-- a traditional Jewish family.
We-- my father worked.
My mother did not.
My, my grandmother, my maternal grandmother, lived with us.
My paternal grandmother was often in our house, although she did not-- she lived in a suburb of Frankfurt where, actually, my father and all the siblings were born on my father's side.
It was called Budesheim-- B-U-D-E-S-H-E-I-M.
We were fairly orthodox-- we observed Shabbat.
We walked to synagogue.
We did not ride.
We were not allowed to do any kind of work on, on, on the Friday night or Saturday, even though my father occasionally had to work, and that was OK.
My father had a-- was in, in textiles.
He traveled and sold textiles, fabrics, or sometimes clothing, items of clothing, specialized in blouses.
But he did a bit of both.
My father, my mother, my brother, and myself.
My brother was one and a half year older than I.
He was born in 1928.
No, we didn't, actually.
We lived in an apartment in a, in a mixed neighborhood.
There were a number of Jews around.
We, we had a synagogue within walking distance, a fairly large one, and a Jewish school, also, within walking distance of my house.
Yes, I did.
No, not really, no.
We went to visit relatives in neighboring areas, but I don't ever recall going on a trip.
My earliest recollections are-- well, first of all, although I didn't know it, I went to a Jewish school because after 1935, Jewish children could not go to public schools.
The public schools were barred to all Jews.
I was fortunate that we had a Jewish school in my city and even in my neighborhood.

Because Jewish children who lived in towns where there were no Jewish schools, unless the local boards of education-- if you call them that-- were willing to donate one classroom in the public school system for all  the Jewish children, these Jewish children could not go to school.
They either had to be sent away and become boarding students or do a great deal of traveling in order to attend classes or else didn't go to school at all.
It was certainly not uncommon.
The earliest signs of anti-Semitism-- I vividly recall signs on the stores.
This, of course, was in '38, probably '37, '38. 'Juden verboten,' meaning Jews are not allowed.
I vividly recall curfews.
Jews could not be out after a certain time.
I remember I used to be very frightened of these curfews.
I wanted to be sure that we-- that the whole family followed these curfews and were not caught [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after, after the curfew hours.

I recall children, gradually, that I used to play with staying away from me and even insulting me, calling me a dirty Jew or singing German jingles that children sing when they play hopscotch, and-- but these jingles turn into anti-Jewish jingles.
And I was deeply hurt.
Yeah, I remember it.
I remember it in German, of course.
Do you want me to say it?
It's [SPEAKING GERMAN].
It's very hard to translate because it rhymes in German.
It doesn't rhyme in English.
It means Jew, shit in the bag.
Shit out of it again.
And you're out.
It's-- kids used to play hopscotch, you know-- and, and, you know, you're in, you're out, or whatever they played.
And this was their way of counting.
It-- what did it mean to me?
It just meant that I wasn't wanted, that I was, you know, no longer part of the crowd, that I was different, that I was somehow tainted.
And, you know, what does it mean to a seven or eight-year-old child?
No.
No, I don't.
Not really.
Not really.
My parents-- I don't ever recall them talking to me about anything of that nature.
I think that in those days, children, you know, were to be seen but not necessarily heard and not to be spoken to in any serious vein.
No, I don't recall them ever discussing any of this.
I only recall them discussing it very late, around the time of Kristallnacht when the real fear emerged.
And in fact, Kristallnacht was the last day I went to school-- or the day after Kristallnacht.
And I went to school only to be, to be told by my teacher that from now on, school, the school would be closed.
We were in the house and I somehow-- I don't know how I knew, but I knew what was going on.
I don't know how.
Did I hear noses?
Maybe I did.
Maybe I didn't.
I really don't remember it that vividly.
I remember the fear.
I remember being terrified.
My father was arrested.
And I already knew that I was leaving.
And I was very excited about leaving.
And I'll tell you a bit more about that later.
And I recall saying to my mother, I will not leave until my father comes back.
And fortunately, because he had a visa, he had an-- I mean, somebody had posted an affidavit for him, they let him go.
And he returned the next day.
It was from the United States.
We had-- there were a lot of relatives.
My mother came from a large family.
My father didn't come from-- you know, from a-- there were four brothers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother was one-- well, also of four.
No, it was only three left.
Two had died.
But there were a lot of cousins on her side, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] dozens of them.
And most of the relatives were able to come.
I think there were enough affidavits posted from a large segment of the family.
And my mother somehow fell through the cracks.
I guess they didn't have enough money anymore.
And we felt that if my father came to the United States, he could then send for her.
It wasn't going to happen, but we didn't know that at the time.
I think he, he got his, his visa already in 1937.
He left in 1940.
He got-- it was very, very difficult to get passage.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not only was it difficult to get a visa, but as the situation became more difficult, another impediment was that there was a great lack of ships.
And so one had to-- you had to wait your turn.
He eventually went to Italy.
I didn't learn this until after the war.
He eventually went to Italy in early 1940 and managed to get a passage out of Italy.
They just came to the house.
They arrested-- it was just a-- they arrested any Jew that they could think of.
And we were lucky they didn't do any damage, do any other damage in the house.
It was not unusual for them to smash the furniture, to steal whatever they wanted to take.
Yes.
Yes.
I, I only remember him being away a day.
And he was able-- I guess they believed him that he had  his visa.
I'm sure he didn't have it with him.
Well, the plan was just that my father would leave and that the rest of us would follow.
The rest included my mother, my brother, myself, and my two grandmothers.
Yeah.
Yeah.

But after Kristallnacht, that changed-- or maybe even before, when the situation became very difficult in Germany, when, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when the curfews not only involved, not only dictated what hours a Jew could be out, it also dictated what stores you could go to, what hours you could go to those stores, what public transportation you could take.
Every move was practically regulated.
And the anti-Semitism became so obvious with slogans posted all over the place.
Most stores had Juden verboten-- Jews forbidden-- with the swastika.
And, and these, these slogans were huge.
They were very large.
No one could-- it was impossible to not notice them.
I think my parents saw the writing on the wall.
And they decided that if they could not leave-- like my mother could not leave-- at least perhaps the children could leave.
Now, I'd like to point out at the time when my parents were making those decisions-- and I'm always amazed that my mother was 33.
She had not yet turned 34.
And my father was 39.
Yes.
My mother had told me that we would be going away.
And I remember I used to love to travel.
I used to be fascinated by trains and train stations.
And I was very excited because I didn't, of course, understand fully the implication of where I was going.
I don't think I even asked.
I just knew that I was going to take a train ride and my brother would be coming with me.
I remember her preparing my, my suitcase with-- buying me new clothes.
And I could-- I was allowed to ask for a new toy, which was very unusual.
In those days that didn't happen very often.
It's so different today, when kids have thousands of toys.
And I recall I asked for a manicure set.
And I had a doll that I loved, and I asked that my doll be packed up.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I, I certainly knew, but I didn't.
I, I knew that I was going somewhere, but I didn't understand the implication of, of leaving.
Did the practice of Judaism in your home remain as observant during this period as it had before?
As far as I know, I think so.
Yeah, we were Kosher, and  we maintained Kashrut.
I did-- I don't believe we-- probably didn't continue attending synagogue.
I really don't remember.
It's very fuzzy.
Well, at that time I was certainly too young, yeah.
No.
I, I-- if I was aware of it, I certainly didn't have any strong feelings about it one way or the other.
I think that basically, personally, I was never very religious.
I mean, I observed what I had to observe, but I'm not sure that it went much beyond that.
Or maybe I'm now projecting back.
I don't know.
We left in November of 1938.
Yeah, my father was still home.
Yes.
They, they lived on whatever money they had.
My mother came from a fairly well-to-do family and had some resources.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father did not, but they must have had some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they must have had enough to survive.
Most Jews did.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he may have gotten some little jobs here and there, but it was very, very difficult.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I, I should mention that my, my brother was, and still is, a handicapped child, a mentally handicapped child, who could not function in a normal environment.
When we, when we lived at home, he had a tutor who came every day to teach him.
And he never attended public-- he never attended any school.
And of course, all this came to an end.
His education literally stopped when he was 10.
He was 10 when we left.
Yes, it was my responsibility, and there was nobody else.
And I think I grew up at that instant.
My, my mother came with us to-- as far as Cologne.
We took the train from Frankfurt to Cologne, which is not very far.
It's maybe 30, 40 minutes train ride.
And that was the end.
And that's when I realized that this trip was not an ordinary trip.
Mm-hmm.
No, it was not dangerous.
I don't think any-- no, the Germans at that time did not mind to see the Jews leave.
In fact, they encouraged it.
Yes, I just-- I never knew which organization, unfortunately.
I've, I've looked.
I've tried to find out from records in Belgium through, through some of the rescuers that I know in Belgium.
But they couldn't locate any records, although records do exist.
But I know there was an organization that arranged this.
I was certainly not the only child on this train.
There were hundreds of us as far as I-- at least that's the way I remember it.
The train trip [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was very difficult, because all of a sudden I realized that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that my mother was not with us and that my father was not with us.

And I also realized that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother was my responsibility, and I felt very strongly about taking care of my brother-- I always have all my life-- and that the future was very unknown.
I was only eight years old, but I, I did realize that.
I knew I was leaving.
I knew exactly what was happening in Germany.
Maybe my parents did talk about it.
I certainly learned about it somewhere.
Of course, I experienced enough of it to, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to be very frightened and to be very hurt.
I-- there was one incident that I'll never forget when-- the way I remember it, a child-- I was outside, and a child threw something at me, either a stone or a piece of glass.
And it hit me in the arm, and I started to bleed.
And I remember bleeding profusely.
My mother said I didn't, but, you know, maybe as a child that's the way I remember it.
And after that, I, I refused to go and play outside.
We played outside a lot.
We didn't have much of a garden.
I don't-- you know, it was a little courtyard in the back, but basically the children played on street.
And after that, I, I refused to go out.
And I would look out of the window and watch other children, but I, myself, was much too afraid and too, too upset with that experience.
That experience will always remain with me.
I was so shocked and so incredulous that, you know, somebody I knew, all of a sudden, would turn against me for no reason, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] except that I-- they knew I was Jewish.
No, no.
No, not that time.
That came into force, I think, around 1942.
You know, I don't know.
Probably just a birth certificate.
I don't think you needed much.
None at all.
None at all.
Unfortunately, I don't remember where we went initially.
We went to some kind of a reception center.
No.
I don't know.
No, I don't-- no, I know it was not in Brussels, but I have no idea where it was.
And I do know that the children were sent to a variety of places-- to private homes, to orphanages, to convents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to whoever would accept a child or children.
They were, they were sent-- you know, I heard about this later.
They were sent to different countries, not necessarily Belgium.
They were sent to France or Holland.
I mean, I, I don't think this was the only transport of children.
There must have been many.
No, no.
Yeah, yeah.
Not very many, but there were some adults who were walking up and down the cars.
But they didn't really do much.
What could they do?
No, she didn't know.
No, she literally-- and I, I think about that a lot.

When someone sends a child away in this country-- say, summer camp or to sleep over, I mean, you make sure that, you know, the camp is a good one, that it has a telephone, that it has proper medical attention, that it's clean and that the children are well fed, that they are properly taken care of, that they're-- you know, that they have positive experiences.
No, my parents knew nothing, nothing.
They didn't know where I would be going.
They had no idea.

All they knew was that it was better to leave than to stay and that no matter what happened on the other side of the border, it would be better for us, for my brother and myself, than staying in, in, in Germany.
And it was my mother who was really the force in, in our household.

And she had a street smartness about her that helped her along many times, including at that time when she, when she understood the importance of sending us away before the war broke out, that she saw the writing on the wall.
I mean, on one hand, they waited much too long.
They should have left in '33, '34, '35.
They didn't do it because, like so many other Germans, they were staunch Germans.
They-- my mother lost a brother in the First World War, you know, had fought for the Kaiser and for the, for Germany.
They were-- I think they were Germans first and Jews second.
And they simply did not believe that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that Hitler was serious about his threats.
Now, many, many did believe it and left, fortunately, but too many did not.
And when, when it came time to leave, unfortunately, most of the doors that these people knocked on were closed.
And this is something that I think about a lot.
It can't have been very long, because I've done this trip many times.
Well, not from Germany, but I've done similar trips.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember.
I seem to remember it took most of the day.
But I, I was in such a-- you know, I, I was so overwhelmed.
I was so upset that time meant nothing.
I was just-- I didn't really care where I was going anymore at that point.
I just wanted to go home.
Yeah.
I thought I would be able to stay with my brother, yeah.
Uh huh.
And then what did happen when you arrived?
Well, we went to this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] place-- whatever it was-- a reception center.
I'll call it, call it that, because I don't know what else to call it.
I think it was a dispersement center.
It was a place where the children came, and from there they were dispersed to different places.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember we slept in huge dormitories-- enormous.
And the-- I think they were nuns who took care of us there.
They came at night and felt your bed.
I'll never forget that.
To see if you were bed wetters.
Yeah, yeah.
It was incredible.
You know, it was very degrading.
And I don't know how long we were there, my brother and I.
He was fairly well behaved there.
I don't recall that we had any problems.
I recall that I-- all I remember of that place was that I used to sit, that we used to sit all day.
We, we were allowed to ask for one item from our suitcase, and I remember I asked for my manicure set.
And we sat around these long, rectangular tables.
At least, that's the way I remember it.
And I remember most of the time, I cried.
I just-- you know, I was so ashamed that I couldn't stop myself.
And I recall that I was the only one crying.
Which, thinking back about it, it seems rather unrealistic.
I'm sure that if I was crying, other kids were crying, too.
And if they weren't crying, then probably I wasn't crying that much either.
I, I don't know.
This is what I remember.
My brother seemed, at that point, not to be a problem.
Eventually, a, a car came to, to pick us up.
I have no idea how long we were  there-- a week, two weeks-- not terribly long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A car came to pick us up and drove us to Brussels, to this orphanage.
Yes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were not-- we were placed not in the same building.
This orphanage was composed of several buildings.
There were about 350 children in the orphanage.
And we were dispersed in different areas.
In French?
[SPEAKING FRENCH]
Orphanage.
It was a home for orphans.
Oh, no, no.
Almost none were Jewish.
No, there were very few Jewish children.
This was a state orphanage.
Most of the children actually were not complete orphans.
Many had one relative alive, either a mother or father.
They came from broken homes.
They came from poor homes.
There was no welfare in those days and certainly not in Belgium.
And if a family was out of work and out of money, then the, the children were very, very often placed in state orphanages.
They were very common.
There were lots of orphanages around.
